title: Quiz: 1/f Noise in Quantum Hardware
description: Test your understanding of 1/f noise physics, its origins, effects on qubit coherence, and mitigation strategies

questions.question: According to the Dutta-Horn model, 1/f noise arises from:

options.text: A single two-level system with a 1/f switching rate
An ensemble of two-level systems with log-uniformly distributed switching rates
Thermal fluctuations in the superconducting ground state
Quantum vacuum fluctuations at low frequencies

questions.explanation: The Dutta-Horn model explains 1/f noise as arising from many two-level fluctuators (TLS), each producing Lorentzian spectrum S ~ tau/(1 + (omega*tau)^2). When the switching rates (1/tau) are log-uniformly distributed (equivalently, activation energies are uniformly distributed), the sum produces 1/f noise in the frequency range between the fastest and slowest fluctuators.

questions.question: What is the characteristic decay shape for pure dephasing caused by 1/f noise?
questions.explanation: 1/f noise produces Gaussian decay at short times because the accumulated phase has a Gaussian distribution with variance proportional to t^2 (with logarithmic corrections). This is characteristic of non-Markovian noise with strong low-frequency components, unlike exponential decay from white (Markovian) noise.

questions.question: For ideal 1/f flux noise, how does T2 scale with the number of pi pulses (n) in a CPMG sequence?
questions.explanation: For ideal 1/f noise (alpha = 1), the decoherence rate scales as 1/n because CPMG with n pulses probes noise at frequency omega_n = n*pi/t. Since S(omega) ~ 1/omega for 1/f noise, T2 ~ 1/S(omega_n) ~ omega_n ~ n. This linear scaling is a diagnostic signature of 1/f noise.

questions.question: Operating a flux-tunable transmon at the 'sweet spot' (Phi = 0) completely eliminates sensitivity to flux noise.
questions.explanation: False! At the sweet spot, the first derivative d(omega)/d(Phi) = 0, which eliminates first-order sensitivity to flux noise. However, second-order sensitivity through d^2(omega)/d(Phi)^2 remains. The qubit is still affected by flux noise, just much less than at non-sweet-spot operating points. Additionally, other noise sources (charge noise, photon noise) may become limiting.

questions.question: The typical amplitude of 1/f flux noise in superconducting qubits is approximately:
questions.explanation: The typical flux noise amplitude in superconducting qubits is 1-10 micro-Phi_0/sqrt(Hz) at 1 Hz. This value has been remarkably consistent across different devices and groups, suggesting a universal origin related to surface spins or defects. This amplitude sets the fundamental limit for T2 in flux-sensitive qubits.

questions.question: How does the CPMG filter function suppress 1/f noise compared to free induction decay (Ramsey)?

options.text: It amplifies high-frequency noise to cancel low-frequency noise
It creates a bandpass filter that rejects DC and low-frequency components
It averages the noise to zero through repeated measurements
It reduces the qubit's coupling to the environment

questions.explanation: The CPMG filter function has zeros at DC and low frequencies, creating an effective bandpass filter. The pi pulses periodically reverse the sign of phase accumulation, causing slow noise components to cancel out. The filter peaks at frequencies omega_n = n*pi/t, where t is the total sequence time and n is the number of pulses.

questions.question: Which of the following are effective strategies for mitigating 1/f noise in superconducting qubits? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Operating at a flux sweet spot where d(omega)/d(Phi) = 0
Using dynamical decoupling sequences like CPMG
Cooling the qubit to lower temperatures
Using tantalum instead of aluminum for capacitor pads to reduce TLS density

questions.explanation: Effective 1/f noise mitigation includes: (a) sweet spot operation to reduce flux sensitivity, (b) dynamical decoupling to filter low-frequency noise, and (d) materials improvements to reduce TLS density. Simply cooling (c) has limited effect because 1/f noise often saturates at low temperatures due to quantum tunneling of TLS defects.

questions.question: In the context of 1/f noise, what does the infrared cutoff frequency represent physically?

options.text: The maximum frequency of the noise spectrum
The inverse of the longest measurement/experiment time

questions.explanation: The infrared (low-frequency) cutoff represents the inverse of the longest relevant timescale in the experiment. Below this frequency, the noise appears as a static offset rather than fluctuations. The 1/f spectrum cannot extend to zero frequency (which would give infinite total noise power), so f_IR ~ 1/t_experiment provides a natural cutoff.
